school: Alchemical / Scientific School — student of Ja'far al-Sadiq

books.title: Kitab al-Kimya (Book of Composition)
books.note: Father of chemistry; developed distillation, crystallization, and calcination — over 3,000 works attributed

books.title: The Works of Geber (Latin translations)
books.note: Medieval Latin translations of alchemical works

quotes: The first essential in chemistry is that you should perform practical work and conduct experiments — for he who performs not practical work nor makes experiments will never attain the least degree of mastery.
Work, work, and yet more work is the only key to the secrets of nature.

school: House of Wisdom, Baghdad — Founded algebra as a mathematical discipline

books.title: Al-Kitab al-Mukhtasar fi Hisab al-Jabr wal-Muqabala (Book of Algebra)
books.note: 'Algebra' comes directly from 'al-jabr' in this title. The word 'algorithm' derives from his Latinised name.

books.title: Kitab Surat al-Ard (Book of the Form of the Earth)
Algebra (Kitab al-Mukhtasar fi Hisab al-Jabr)
books.note: The book that named algebra — English translation

quotes: I have composed this work on calculation by completion and reduction, confining it to what is easiest and most useful — such as men constantly require in cases of inheritance, legacies, and trade.
The numbers required in calculation are of three kinds: roots, squares, and simple numbers.

school: Abbasid Scientific Court — most influential Islamic astrologer; his Latin translations shaped European medieval astronomy

books.title: Introductorium in Astronomiam (Introduction to Astronomy)
books.note: Translated as 'Albumasar' in Latin; most influential Islamic astrology text in medieval Europe

school: House of Wisdom, Baghdad
quotes: The science of the stars is among the most noble — for it concerns itself with the greatest of God's creations.
The circumference of the earth, when measured, reveals the precision of its Creator.

school: House of Wisdom, Baghdad
House of Wisdom, Baghdad
House of Wisdom, Baghdad

books.title: On the Difference between the Spirit and the Soul

school: House of Wisdom, Baghdad
Islamic Medicine, Baghdad — Founded empirical clinical medicine through patient observation

books.title: Al-Hawi al-Kabir (The Comprehensive Book of Medicine — 25 vols)
books.note: Largest medical encyclopaedia ever compiled — used in European medical schools for centuries.

books.title: Kitab al-Jadari wal-Hasba (Smallpox and Measles)
books.note: First accurate clinical distinction between smallpox and measles in medical history.

quotes: The most excellent doctor is he who can bring hope to the patient’s heart.
Truth in medicine is a goal that cannot be fully attained — the art is long and life is short.
I prefer to verify by experiment rather than accept authority alone.
The most excellent doctor is he who can bring hope to the patient’s heart.
Truth in medicine is a goal that cannot be fully attained — the art is long and life is short.
I prefer to verify by experiment rather than accept authority alone.

school: A ninth- and tenth-century astronomer and mathematician from Harran, he refined Ptolemy's astronomical observations with unprecedented accuracy, determining the length of the solar year to within two minutes of the modern value. His Kitab al-Zij (Astronomical Tables) corrected several of Ptolemy's calculations and introduced the use of trigonometric functions into astronomy. His work was translated into Latin and profoundly influenced medieval European astronomers including Copernicus.

books.title: Wikipedia — Al-Battani
books.note: Wikipedia article

quotes: The motions of the heavens are governed by laws as fixed as the laws of arithmetic.
Observation corrects theory — and theory without observation is merely poetry.

school: Buyid Court

books.title: Book of Fixed Stars (Kitab Suwar al-Kawakib)

school: A tenth-century Andalusian physician and surgeon from Cordoba, he authored Kitab al-Tasrif (The Method of Medicine), a thirty-volume medical encyclopaedia that became the standard surgical reference in both the Islamic world and medieval Europe. The surgical volume described over two hundred instruments, many of which he designed himself, and covered procedures from cauterisation to cataract surgery. He is widely regarded as the father of modern surgery.

books.title: Albucasis on Surgery and Instruments
books.note: Revolutionary illustrated surgical manual — English

quotes: The root and foundation of medicine is compassion for the patient — the physician who lacks it should not be trusted.
I have explained the art of surgery in detail never before attempted by any physician before me.

school: Cairo — Founded empirical optics and the experimental method; influenced Roger Bacon, Kepler and Newton

books.title: Kitab al-Manazir (Book of Optics — 7 vols)
books.note: Founded the science of optics; proved that light enters the eye (not emanates from it). Directly influenced European Renaissance science.

books.title: Wikipedia — Ibn al-Haytham
books.note: Wikipedia article

books.title: Book of Optics (Kitab al-Manazir)
books.note: Foundational work on optics and vision — English

quotes: The duty of the man who investigates the writings of scientists is to make himself an enemy of all that he reads.
The duty of the man who investigates the writings of scientists, if learning the truth is his goal, is to make himself an enemy of all that he reads.
Truth is sought for its own sake. Those engaged in the quest for truth are not interested in what they already presume to know.
The seeker after truth is not one who studies the writings of the ancients and follows his natural inclination — but one who suspects his own faith in them.
If learning the truth is the scientist's goal, then he must make himself the enemy of all that he reads.

school: Ghaznavid Court / Universal Scholarship — wrote 146 works across 11 disciplines

books.title: Kitab al-Hind (Book of India — Indica)
books.note: First systematic comparative study of a non-Islamic civilization — unparalleled cross-cultural scholarship.

books.title: Wikipedia — Al-Biruni
books.note: Wikipedia article

books.title: Kitab al-Hind (Alberuni's India)
books.note: Comprehensive study of Indian science, philosophy and culture

books.title: Alberuni's India (Kitab al-Hind)
books.note: Classic study of Indian civilization — English

books.title: Book of Instruction in Astrology
books.note: Mathematical and astronomical manual — English

quotes: I shall take refuge in the character of a chronicler, and describe what I found, without adding any judgment of my own.
Facts do not perish, even when those who carry them pass away.
We must not allow the love of what is familiar to blind us to what is true.

school: An eleventh-century Andalusian astronomer and instrument maker from Toledo, he developed the universal astrolabe (the Saphaea), which could be used at any latitude, a significant advance over earlier designs. He compiled the Toledan Tables, the most accurate astronomical tables of his era, which were used across Europe for over two centuries. His observations of the motion of the solar apogee were the first to demonstrate that it moves slowly relative to the fixed stars.
quotes: I corrected Ptolemy not out of disrespect — but because the observations demanded it.
The Toledo Tables are my gift to every navigator who will come after me.
Precision in astronomy is precision in worship — for we are describing God’s creation.

school: A twelfth-century Andalusian physician from Seville, he came from a distinguished family of physicians and is known for his clinical text al-Taysir fi al-Mudawat wa-l-Tadbir (The Facilitation of Treatment). He was among the first physicians to describe conditions such as pericarditis and mediastinal tumours, and he advocated for experimental methods in medicine. His close collaboration with the philosopher Ibn Rushd (Averroes) produced complementary works on medical theory and practice.

books.title: Kitab al-Taysir fi al-Mudawat wa al-Tadbir

quotes: The physician who treats himself has a fool for a patient.
Clinical observation is the only trustworthy foundation for the art of medicine.
The physician who treats himself has a fool for a patient.
Clinical observation is the only trustworthy foundation for the art of medicine.

school: Artuqid Court Engineering — chief engineer at the Artuqid court in Diyarbakir

books.title: Kitab fi Ma'rifat al-Hiyal al-Handasiyya (Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices)
books.note: Describes 50 mechanical devices including water clocks, automata, and pumps — the Kitab is the founding text of robotics

books.title: Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices
books.note: Landmark medieval engineering manual — English

quotes: The craftsman who understands the principles behind his craft will surpass the one who merely follows instructions.
Every machine is a kind of philosophy — it embodies a theory of how things work.
The greatest invention is the one that reduces human burden without reducing human dignity.

school: A thirteenth-century Andalusian botanist and pharmacologist from Malaga, he authored Kitab al-Jami (The Comprehensive Book on Simple Drugs), the largest and most comprehensive botanical and pharmaceutical encyclopaedia of the medieval period. The work catalogued over 1,400 plants, drugs, and foods, drawing on personal fieldwork conducted across North Africa and the eastern Mediterranean. His systematic approach to botanical classification and drug testing represented a major advance in pharmacological science.

books.title: Kitab al-Jami li-Mufradat al-Adwiya wa al-Aghdhiya

quotes: God has placed in every plant a remedy — the physician’s task is to find it.
I have travelled every land not for adventure but for knowledge of what grows in it.
The herbalist who does not travel is like a scholar who does not read beyond one book.

school: Islamic Science and Philosophy — survived the Mongol invasions by serving Hulagu Khan, then established the Maragha Observatory (1259), the world's greatest scientific institution of its era

books.title: Zij-i Ilkhani (Ilkhanid Astronomical Tables)
books.note: Astronomical tables used across the Islamic world and reaching Renaissance Europe — Copernicus's heliocentric model built on Tusi's work.

books.title: Memoir on Astronomy (al-Tadhkira fi Ilm al-Hay'a)
books.note: Influential astronomical treatise — English

quotes: Knowledge is the greatest of all fortresses — it neither crumbles nor is captured.
The universe obeys its own laws, which we must struggle to understand.

school: A thirteenth-century Syrian physician and polymath based in Cairo, he is best known for his discovery of pulmonary circulation, describing how blood passes from the right to the left ventricle of the heart via the lungs, three centuries before William Harvey. He detailed this finding in his Sharh Tashrih al-Qanun (Commentary on the Anatomy of the Canon), challenging Galen's long-accepted model of the cardiovascular system. He was also a prolific author on jurisprudence, theology, and philosophy.
quotes: The blood must have a means of passage to the left cavity — and that passage is through the lung.
I have not omitted anything that might benefit the student of medicine, nor added anything that could mislead him.

school: Maragha school — student of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi; explained the rainbow; major work on optics, astronomy and medicine

books.title: Nihayat al-Idrak
books.url: ,note:"Major work on astronomy — first correct explanation of the rainbow

school: A thirteenth- and fourteenth-century Moroccan mathematician and astronomer from Marrakesh, he authored numerous works on arithmetic, algebra, and astronomy. His Talkhis A'mal al-Hisab (Summary of Arithmetic Operations) introduced innovative methods for calculation and became a standard mathematical textbook across North Africa. He also contributed to the development of algebraic notation and compiled astronomical tables used in the western Islamic world.
Maragha School
quotes: Light bends when it enters water — and in that bending is the secret of the rainbow.
I confirmed Ibn al-Haytham’s optics by experiment — confirmation is the honour due to good science.
The colours of the rainbow are not painted by chance — they are a proof of mathematical order.

school: Samarkand Observatory (Ulugh Beg)
Samarkand Observatory
quotes: The astronomer who does not question his own measurements has stopped being an astronomer.
Mathematics is the language in which God wrote the motion of the stars.

school: Ottoman Navy
quotes: The sea gives generously to those who respect her — and takes everything from those who do not.
Every map is a theory of the world — draw it well.
The navigator who does not know the stars has already lost his way before he sets sail.

school: Istanbul Observatory
quotes: The observatory is not a place of arrogance — it is a place of submission to what the heavens reveal.
Every star that is measured glorifies God in the language of mathematics.
Precision is an act of piety — the scientist who is careless has not understood his task.

school: Safavid scholarship — polymath genius of architecture, mathematics, astronomy, poetry and fiqh

books.title: Kashkul (The Begging Bowl)
books.url: ,note:"Encyclopedic anthology of science, literature and mysticism

school: Ottoman mathematics — the most accomplished Ottoman mathematician; introduced modern European mathematics to the Ottoman system

books.title: Maktubat-i Mazhari
books.url: ,note:"Letters — one of the most read Naqshbandi spiritual letter collections in South Asia

school: A Pakistani theoretical physicist who shared the 1979 Nobel Prize in Physics for his contribution to the electroweak unification theory, becoming the first Muslim Nobel laureate in science. He founded the International Centre for Theoretical Physics (ICTP) in Trieste, Italy, to provide scientists from developing countries access to advanced research facilities. His lifelong mission was to promote scientific excellence in the Muslim world, and he advocated tirelessly for investment in fundamental research.
